nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated; typically found singly or in pairs over silty flat sand patches or rubble substrates near outer reef edges and coral drop-offs at depths of 1–45 m.

diet.notes: Feeds primarily by sifting mouthfuls of sand to extract small invertebrates, crustaceans, copepods, amphipods, mysis shrimp, and brine shrimp. Has been documented accepting frozen mysis, bloodworms, and prepared carnivore foods. Tends to require frequent feeding — daily at minimum — and benefits from a well-established sandbed rich in microfauna.

summary: A sand-sifting saltwater goby known for its pale body marked with two bold black horizontal stripes. Generally considered reef-safe and peaceful; tends to be territorial toward its own kind unless kept as a mated pair.
careNotes:
The Sleeper Railway Glider Goby is generally considered an intermediate-level species despite being rated 'beginner' or 'easy' by several hobby sources; one specialist source (ReefApp) rates it as requiring experience and preparation, particularly due to its demanding feeding needs. It sifts sand continuously to feed, so a deep, fine-grained sandbed with an established population of copepods and amphipods is commonly advised. Has been reported to jump from open-top aquariums; a tightly fitting lid is generally recommended. Best kept singly or as a bonded pair — have been known to be highly aggressive toward other individuals of the same species. Rarely bothers other tank inhabitants. The sand-sifting behavior can occasionally blanket nearby corals with sediment, which is worth noting when planning aquascape placement.

Adult size disagreement across sources: LiveAquaria reports 15.24 cm (6 in), Bay Bridge Aquarium 15.875 cm (6.25 in), BlueZoo reports 25.4 cm (10 in), FishBase and ReefApp both cite 25 cm maximum. The lower hobby figures may reflect typical aquarium specimens; the higher scientific figures reflect recorded maximum length. A range of 15–25 cm is used here.

Minimum tank volume disagreement across sources: Bay Bridge Aquarium recommends 25 gal (94.6 L), LiveAquaria 30 gal (113.6 L), BlueZoo 50 gal (189.3 L), ReefApp 600 L (158 gal). Average across all four sources is approximately 249 L. The ReefApp figure (~600 L) is substantially higher than the others, likely reflecting the species' sand-sifting territory requirements at full size. Reviewer should assess an appropriate minimum — a value in the 113–190 L range (30–50 gal) may be more practical for most hobby situations.

Care level disagreement: Three major hobby retailers (LiveAquaria, Bay Bridge, BlueZoo) classify this species as 'beginner'/'easy'; ReefApp classifies it as requiring experience. The intermediate designation here reflects a middle-ground accounting for the species' sensitivity to ammonia, nitrite, and inadequate sandbed microlife, which can make it challenging for new aquarists despite its peaceful temperament.
breedingNotes: Has been documented forming monogamous mated pairs in the wild and is a mound-building species. Captive breeding information is not widely available in hobby sources. Has been known to spawn in aquariums, though successful larval rearing is reportedly difficult.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing, care level, tank size, diet, temperament, reef safety. Reports adult size as 10 inches (25.4 cm) and min tank 50 gal (189.3 L).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, taxonomy (Gobiidae, Gobiiformes), maximum length 25 cm, native range (Indo-West Pacific), temperature 22–27°C, and monogamous pairing behavior.

additional.name: Bay Bridge Aquarium
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (6.25 in / 15.9 cm), temperature (75–82°F / 23.9–27.8°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), care level (easy), and reef safe (yes). Min tank 25 gal (94.6 L).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (25 cm), native range (Indo-West Pacific), reef safe status ('with caution' due to sand-sifting potentially covering corals), care level ('experience required' — highest rating among sources), and min tank 600 L. This source's min tank figure is a significant outlier vs. other sources.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been documented forming monogamous mated pairs in the wild and is a mound-building species. Captive breeding information is not widely available in hobby sources. Has been known to spawn in aquariums, though successful larval rearing is reportedly difficult.
